meta.title: SF-SPAA-5: Account Governance, Cost Control, and Recovery Administration

questions.stem: Which organization-level role can create and manage accounts in a Snowflake organization?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN is an account-level role focused on creating and managing databases, warehouses, and other objects within a single account — it cannot create or manage other accounts.

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. ORGADMIN is the special organization-level role that can create new Snowflake accounts, view account usage across the organization, and manage organization-level settings. It is the only role scoped at the organization level.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN is an account-level role for creating and managing users and roles within the account, not for cross-account organization management.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN manages grants, network policies, and security objects within a single account, not across the organization.

questions.stem: Which command changes the active role for the current Snowflake session?

options.text: ALTER SESSION SET ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. `ALTER SESSION SET ROLE` is not valid Snowflake syntax — session parameters are set with `ALTER SESSION SET` but role switching uses `USE ROLE`.

options.text: GRANT ROLE ... TO SESSION
options.explanation: is incorrect. `GRANT ROLE` assigns a role to a user or another role; it does not switch the active role in a session.

options.text: USE ROLE
options.explanation: is correct. `USE ROLE <role_name>` is the standard SQL command that changes the primary active role for the current session. The new role takes effect immediately for subsequent statements.

options.text: SWITCH ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SWITCH ROLE` is not a Snowflake SQL command.

questions.stem: A company wants separate billing, administration, and security boundaries for two business units. What is the best high-level design?

options.text: Two databases in one account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Two databases in one account share the same billing, the same administrative hierarchy, and the same account-level security settings — they do not provide true boundary separation.

options.text: Two schemas in one database
options.explanation: is incorrect. Two schemas share even more than two databases do — they share the same database, account, and all account-level policies and billing.

options.text: Two warehouses in one account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouses are compute objects; separate warehouses do not create billing or administrative separation — all usage still appears on one account invoice.

options.text: Two accounts within one organization
options.explanation: is correct. Separate accounts within a Snowflake organization provide complete billing isolation (each account has its own credit consumption tracked separately), distinct administrative boundaries (each account has its own ACCOUNTADMIN), and strong security separation.

questions.stem: Which system-defined role is primarily associated with creating and managing users and roles?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN focuses on creating and managing database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses) rather than user identity management.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN has the broadest privileges in the account and can manage users, but it is far more powerful than necessary for this purpose and should be used sparingly.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. USERADMIN is the built-in role specifically designed for creating users and roles and managing role grants. It is the recommended role for day-to-day identity management tasks.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is the lowest-privilege role that every user receives by default — it has no administrative authority.

questions.stem: What is a primary use case for account parameters in Snowflake?

options.text: Publishing data to the Snowflake Marketplace
options.explanation: is incorrect. Marketplace listings are managed through the Snowflake Marketplace product and collaboration features, not account parameters.

options.text: Converting permanent tables to external tables in bulk
options.explanation: is incorrect. Table type conversion uses DDL statements (CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE, etc.), not account parameters.

options.text: Reducing warehouse size automatically after every query
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse sizing after queries is not controlled by account parameters; the closest feature is AUTO_SUSPEND, which is a warehouse property.

options.text: Configuring account-wide defaults such as session behavior and security settings
options.explanation: is correct. Account parameters control account-wide defaults — for example, default warehouse, session timeout, date format, and authentication settings — that apply to all users unless overridden at the user or session level.

questions.stem: Which feature helps analyze usage across multiple accounts in the same Snowflake organization?

options.text: Snowpipe Streaming channels
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpipe Streaming channels are a data ingestion mechanism; they are unrelated to usage reporting.

options.text: Temporary internal stages
options.explanation: is incorrect. Temporary stages are objects used for staging data files; they provide no cross-account usage reporting capability.

options.text: Micro-partition pruning statistics per account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Micro-partition pruning statistics are a storage-layer optimization metric within a single account, not an organization-level usage reporting feature.

options.text: Organization usage views in the SNOWFLAKE shared database
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake exposes cross-account usage data through the ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema in the shared SNOWFLAKE database. An administrator with the ORGADMIN role can query these views to analyze credit consumption, storage, and other metrics across all accounts in the organization.

questions.stem: What is a failover group used for in Snowflake?

options.text: Replicating supported objects across accounts and enabling disaster-recovery failover
options.explanation: is correct. A failover group is a Snowflake object that groups databases and account-level objects for cross-account replication. It also enables a target account to be promoted to primary, which is the disaster recovery (failover) use case.

options.text: Loading files from external cloud storage into Snowflake stages
options.explanation: is incorrect. Loading files from cloud storage uses COPY INTO or Snowpipe with stages and storage integrations — not failover groups.

options.text: Applying dynamic data masking policies to sensitive columns
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dynamic data masking uses masking policies applied to table columns, not failover groups.

options.text: Defining IP-based network access rules for user authentication
options.explanation: is incorrect. IP-based access rules are defined using network policies, not failover groups.

questions.reference: [Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the principle of least privilege in Snowflake administration?

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to every analyst to avoid access blockers
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting ACCOUNTADMIN broadly is the opposite of least privilege — it gives every analyst full administrative power over the account, including billing and security configuration.

options.text: Grant only the minimum roles and privileges needed for each job function
options.explanation: is correct. The principle of least privilege means users and service accounts receive only the access required to perform their specific tasks — nothing more. This limits blast radius if credentials are compromised and reduces audit surface.

options.text: Assign SYSADMIN to all service accounts by default
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN can create and manage all databases and warehouses, which is far more than most service accounts need.

options.text: Avoid roles entirely and grant privileges directly to individual users permanently
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting directly to individual users bypasses role-based management, making privilege reviews and revocation extremely difficult to maintain at scale.

questions.stem: A new Snowflake account must follow enterprise naming conventions and have common governance policies from day one. Which administrative approach is most effective?

options.text: Rely on each team to configure their own settings after they onboard
options.explanation: is incorrect. Relying on teams to self-configure leads to inconsistent naming, missing policies, and governance gaps that are expensive to remediate later.

options.text: Disable RBAC until all projects are onboarded, then enable it
options.explanation: is incorrect. Disabling RBAC is not a valid Snowflake configuration option, and delaying access controls exposes the account to misuse from the start.

options.text: Manually rename objects after deployment to match conventions
options.explanation: is incorrect. Renaming objects after deployment risks breaking pipelines and references, and does not address policy gaps.

options.text: Standardize provisioning scripts and baseline configuration applied at account creation time
options.explanation: is correct. Running standardized provisioning scripts (IaC or Terraform, for example) that apply naming conventions and baseline policies at account creation time ensures consistency and prevents drift before teams start using the account.

questions.stem: Which role should generally be used sparingly because it combines broad administrative powers across the Snowflake account?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNTADMIN is the most powerful system-defined role. It has full control over billing, security, and all account objects. Snowflake recommends tightly restricting who holds it and not using it for day-to-day tasks.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN is scoped to user and role management and does not have broad account-level powers like reading billing data or overriding all security settings.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is the least-privileged role that all users receive automatically; it has no special administrative authority.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN is powerful for object management but cannot access billing data or manage users, so it is less concerning than ACCOUNTADMIN.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a Snowflake reader account?

options.text: To allow data consumers without their own Snowflake account to query shared data
options.explanation: is correct. A reader account is a managed account created by a data provider so that external consumers — who do not have their own Snowflake subscription — can access shared data. The provider pays for compute credits the reader account uses.

options.text: To replace virtual warehouses and provide dedicated compute for heavy ETL jobs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader accounts still use virtual warehouses for compute; they do not replace or eliminate warehouses.

options.text: To manage cross-region replication schedules for failover groups
options.explanation: is incorrect. Replication and failover group schedules are managed through the primary and secondary account configuration, not through reader accounts.

options.text: To create and enforce external network rules for outbound traffic
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network rules and network policies govern inbound/outbound traffic and are configured separately from reader accounts.

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that are generally true about Snowflake account architecture. (Select TWO)

options.text: Databases and warehouses are separate objects so storage and compute can scale independently
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's core architectural principle is the separation of storage and compute. Databases hold data in cloud storage, and virtual warehouses provide compute — they scale and are priced independently.

options.text: Virtual warehouses store table data permanently alongside query results
options.explanation: is incorrect. Virtual warehouses are pure compute clusters; they do not store table data. Table data lives in Snowflake's columnar cloud storage layer.

options.text: Roles are central to RBAC-based administration
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's security model is built on Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). Privileges are granted to roles, which are then granted to users.

options.text: Users query data only through organization-level roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Users query data using account-level roles (custom or system-defined), not organization-level roles. ORGADMIN is an org-level role but does not grant data query access.

questions.stem: A company needs business continuity for a set of databases plus some account objects in another region. Which Snowflake feature family is most relevant?

options.text: Search optimization service
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search optimization service improves selective query performance on large tables; it has nothing to do with cross-region replication or disaster recovery.

options.text: Dynamic data masking policies
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dynamic data masking governs column visibility based on role context — it does not replicate data or provide disaster recovery.

options.text: Tasks and streams for pipeline orchestration
options.explanation: is incorrect. Tasks and streams are used for change data capture and pipeline orchestration within a single account, not for cross-region failover.

options.text: Replication and failover groups
options.explanation: is correct. Replication groups and failover groups are designed for cross-region business continuity. They replicate databases and account-level objects (roles, integrations, policies) to a secondary account and support failover to that secondary.

questions.reference: [Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: Why might an administrator apply tags to objects at the account level?

options.text: To increase warehouse CPU speed for tagged objects
options.explanation: is incorrect. Tags are metadata objects; they have no effect on warehouse compute speed or hardware allocation.

options.text: To force every query on tagged tables into the result cache
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result caching is based on query identity and data freshness — tags on tables do not influence cache behavior.

options.text: To classify objects for governance, discovery, and policy-driven management
options.explanation: is correct. Tags enable data classification so administrators can identify sensitive objects, drive masking and row-access policies based on tag values, and produce governance reports (for example, which tables are tagged PII).

options.text: To create users automatically whenever a new table is tagged
options.explanation: is incorrect. Tags are passive metadata; they do not trigger automated user creation or any other object creation.

questions.stem: Which object scope is most appropriate for setting a policy that should apply to every session in the account unless overridden more specifically?

options.text: Single worksheet
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Snowsight worksheet is a UI artifact and does not define persistent account-wide policies.

options.text: One SQL statement via SET command
options.explanation: is incorrect. A single SQL statement's session-level parameter setting (using the `SET` command) applies only to that session or even less broadly — it is the narrowest scope.

options.text: Account
options.explanation: is correct. Account-level parameters and policies apply to all sessions account-wide by default. Snowflake uses a hierarchy where account settings are the broadest scope, overridden by user-level, then session-level settings.

options.text: A file format object
options.explanation: is incorrect. File format objects define how data files are parsed during load or unload; they are not a scope for session policy settings.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a Snowflake resource monitor?

options.text: To store query result sets permanently for auditing
options.explanation: is incorrect. Persisted query results are managed by Snowflake's result cache feature, not resource monitors.

options.text: To replicate databases to a secondary account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Database replication is handled by replication groups and failover groups, not resource monitors.

options.text: To track and control credit consumption with thresholds and optional automated actions
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitors set credit usage quotas on warehouses or the account. When consumption reaches defined thresholds, Snowflake can notify administrators and optionally suspend warehouses to prevent cost overruns.

options.text: To enforce network access policies on warehouse traffic
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network access is controlled by network policies, not resource monitors.

questions.stem: Which warehouse property automatically reduces cost by stopping idle compute after a defined period of inactivity?

options.text: DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS
options.explanation: is incorrect. DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls how long Time Travel historical data is kept on tables — it is a storage parameter, not a compute cost control.

options.text: CHANGE_TRACKING
options.explanation: is incorrect. CHANGE_TRACKING enables streams to detect row-level changes on a table; it has no effect on warehouse suspension or compute costs.

options.text: MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL
options.explanation: is incorrect. MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL limits how many SQL statements can execute concurrently before queuing, which affects throughput behavior but does not suspend idle warehouses.

options.text: AUTO_SUSPEND
options.explanation: is correct. AUTO_SUSPEND is a warehouse setting that specifies the number of seconds of idle time after which Snowflake automatically suspends the warehouse, stopping credit consumption until the next query arrives.

questions.stem: What does multi-cluster warehouse scaling primarily help address?

options.text: Spikes in concurrent query demand
options.explanation: is correct. Multi-cluster warehouses automatically add or remove compute clusters to handle spikes in concurrent query demand. When many queries arrive simultaneously and queue, additional clusters start to absorb the load.

options.text: Permanent data retention compliance requirements
options.explanation: is incorrect. Data retention compliance is managed through Time Travel and Fail-safe settings on tables, not through multi-cluster warehouse configuration.

options.text: User password policy enforcement at login
options.explanation: is incorrect. Password policies are account-level security objects applied to users — they have nothing to do with warehouse cluster settings.

options.text: Cross-region database replication latency
options.explanation: is incorrect. Cross-region replication latency is a function of the replication group refresh schedule and network, not warehouse cluster count.

questions.stem: A dashboard workload has many short, repetitive queries. Which Snowflake capability may reduce repeated compute work when the exact same query result is still valid?

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy controls which IP addresses can connect to Snowflake — it has no effect on query execution or caching.

options.text: Masking policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A masking policy obfuscates sensitive column values based on role context — it does not optimize repeated query execution.

options.text: Result cache
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's result cache stores query results for up to 24 hours. If the same query is re-submitted and the underlying data has not changed, Snowflake returns the cached result without executing on the warehouse, eliminating compute cost.

options.text: Storage integration
options.explanation: is incorrect. A storage integration configures Snowflake's access to external cloud storage for loading or unloading data — unrelated to caching repeated queries.

questions.stem: Which view family is most useful when investigating historical query execution details such as elapsed time and bytes scanned?

options.text: LOGIN_HISTORY only
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication events (successful and failed logins), not query execution metrics.

options.text: STAGES view only
options.explanation: is incorrect. The STAGES view in INFORMATION_SCHEMA lists stage objects; it does not contain query execution history.

options.text: QUERY_HISTORY and related ACCOUNT_USAGE views
options.explanation: is correct. The QUERY_HISTORY view (and related ACCOUNT_USAGE views such as QUERY_HISTORY_BY_*) captures elapsed time, bytes scanned, credits used, warehouse used, and execution status for historical queries — the primary resource for query performance diagnostics.

options.text: WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY is the only possible source
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY tracks warehouse start, stop, and resize events — not individual query metrics.

questions.stem: What is a likely reason to resize a warehouse upward temporarily?

options.text: To provide more compute resources for heavier workloads or to reduce query duration
options.explanation: is correct. Warehouse size controls the amount of compute (CPU, memory) available. Increasing the size temporarily — for example during a large batch load or complex analytical query — allocates more resources and typically reduces execution time.

options.text: To extend the Time Travel retention period on tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention is controlled by the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS parameter on database/schema/table objects, not by warehouse size.

options.text: To create users in bulk more quickly
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating users is a metadata DDL operation that does not depend on warehouse size — it uses no warehouse compute at all.

options.text: To decrease Fail-safe storage charges
options.explanation: is incorrect. Fail-safe storage charges depend on how much data is in permanent tables, not on warehouse size.

questions.stem: Which statement about warehouse suspension is correct?

options.text: Suspending a warehouse stops compute charges for running clusters until the warehouse is resumed
options.explanation: is correct. When a warehouse is suspended, its virtual compute clusters stop running and Snowflake stops billing for compute credits. Table data in cloud storage is unaffected; the warehouse resumes and reloads cache on the next query.

options.text: Suspending a warehouse deletes cached local disk data permanently from the underlying tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse suspension clears the local disk (SSD) cache on those clusters, but table data is stored in Snowflake's central cloud storage — not on the warehouse — so no table data is deleted.

options.text: Suspending a warehouse removes all RBAC grants associated with that warehouse object
options.explanation: is incorrect. Suspending a warehouse has no effect on RBAC grants. Grants on the warehouse object persist regardless of its running state.

options.text: Suspending a warehouse automatically disables all replication groups globally
options.explanation: is incorrect. Replication groups operate independently of any individual warehouse's suspend state. Suspension of a warehouse does not affect replication.

questions.stem: A team experiences long queue times even though individual queries complete quickly. What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: Time Travel retention is configured too high, delaying query scheduling
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention affects storage cost and historical query capability; it does not cause active query queue times.

options.text: The account has too many object tags, slowing the metadata service
options.explanation: is incorrect. Object tags are passive metadata and have no effect on query execution or queueing behavior.

options.text: File format objects are missing compression codecs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Missing compression on file formats affects load/unload efficiency, not the execution queue for analytical SQL queries.

options.text: Warehouse concurrency is insufficient for the workload's simultaneous query volume
options.explanation: is correct. When queries are fast individually but queue for a long time, the bottleneck is concurrency — more simultaneous queries are arriving than the warehouse can execute at once. Adding clusters (multi-cluster) or a second dedicated warehouse addresses this.

questions.stem: Which administrative action best supports chargeback reporting by business unit?

options.text: Apply consistent tags or naming conventions to warehouses and analyze ACCOUNT_USAGE views by those groupings
options.explanation: is correct. Assigning tags (e.g., cost_center=Finance) to warehouses or using consistent warehouse naming conventions allows administrators to join warehouse credit usage from ACCOUNT_USAGE views with those tags to produce chargeback reports per business unit.

options.text: Disable all result caching to ensure every query is reflected in credit usage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Disabling result caching would increase compute costs unnecessarily and does not improve the granularity of chargeback attribution — warehouse-level credit reporting is already available.

options.text: Grant every team ACCOUNTADMIN so they can view their own billing data
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting ACCOUNTADMIN broadly violates least privilege and exposes all account settings and billing data to every team — that is a governance failure, not a chargeback solution.

options.text: Store all business unit data in a single temporary schema for centralized reporting
options.explanation: is incorrect. Putting all data in one temporary schema prevents object-level attribution and makes it harder, not easier, to separate costs by business unit.

questions.stem: Select TWO actions that commonly help control warehouse compute cost. (Select TWO)

options.text: Configure AUTO_SUSPEND to stop idle warehouses promptly
options.explanation: is correct. Configuring AUTO_SUSPEND ensures that warehouses stop accumulating credits when they are idle, which is one of the most effective warehouse cost controls.

options.text: Use resource monitor thresholds to cap and alert on credit consumption
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitors with threshold-based notifications and optional SUSPEND actions provide a safety net that prevents unexpected credit overruns at the warehouse or account level.

options.text: Default every warehouse to the largest available size
options.explanation: is incorrect. Defaulting to the largest warehouse size maximizes credit consumption and is the opposite of cost control. Warehouse size should match workload requirements.

options.text: Disable QUERY_HISTORY retention to reduce storage overhead
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY data is stored in Snowflake's metadata layer at no additional cost to the customer; disabling it would remove diagnostic capability without reducing warehouse compute spend.

questions.stem: Why might an administrator inspect warehouse load metrics over time?

options.text: To rename schemas automatically based on utilization
options.explanation: is incorrect. Schema renaming is a DDL operation with no connection to warehouse load metrics — and it cannot be automated based on warehouse utilization.

options.text: To convert permanent tables to transient tables based on historical load
options.explanation: is incorrect. Decisions about table type (permanent vs. transient) are based on data lifecycle and cost requirements, not on warehouse load.

options.text: To understand utilization and concurrency patterns and determine if size or multi-cluster settings should change
options.explanation: is correct. Reviewing warehouse load over time reveals whether the warehouse is consistently over-utilized (suggesting a size increase or multi-cluster is needed), under-utilized (suggesting a size decrease), or experiencing concurrency spikes (suggesting auto-scale configuration).

options.text: To grant ownership on internal stages to analyst roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting ownership on stages is an access control decision, not driven by warehouse utilization data.

questions.stem: What is the main difference between scaling up and scaling out for Snowflake warehouses?

options.text: Scaling up increases the number of clusters; scaling out increases per-cluster memory
options.explanation: is incorrect. This reverses the definitions: scaling up changes warehouse size (not cluster count), and scaling out adds clusters (not per-cluster memory separately).

options.text: There is no practical difference — both increase total credits consumed equally
options.explanation: is incorrect. They have different effects and different cost profiles. Scaling up is better for slow individual queries; scaling out is better for concurrent workload volume.

options.text: Scaling up changes warehouse size for more per-query resources; scaling out adds clusters to handle more concurrent queries
options.explanation: is correct. Scaling up means choosing a larger warehouse size (XS → S → M → L → XL, etc.), which gives each individual query more compute power and memory. Scaling out means adding more clusters (multi-cluster mode) so that more queries can run simultaneously without queuing.

options.text: Scaling up changes RBAC role assignments; scaling out transfers object ownership
options.explanation: is incorrect. Neither scaling dimension has any effect on RBAC role assignments or object ownership.

questions.stem: Which privilege model is central to Snowflake security administration?

options.text: Role-based access control (RBAC)
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake security is built primarily on RBAC. Privileges are granted to roles, roles are granted to users or other roles, and the active role determines what actions a user can take. Snowflake also blends elements of DAC (object owners can grant privileges) but RBAC is the core model.

options.text: Discretionary ACLs with no role concept
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not use a purely discretionary ACL model with no roles. Roles are fundamental to how access is structured and delegated.

options.text: Mandatory access control by data sensitivity classification only
options.explanation: is incorrect. While Snowflake supports data classification with tags, its security model is not based solely on sensitivity classification in the style of mandatory access control (MAC).

options.text: Network segmentation only
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network segmentation (via network policies) is an additional security control but is not the core privilege model for object-level access.

questions.stem: What does the `OWNERSHIP` privilege on a Snowflake object imply?

options.text: The role can query the object but cannot alter or drop it
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP is not a read-only grant — it implies full control. SELECT alone grants query access without administrative rights.

options.text: The role automatically inherits ACCOUNTADMIN for that object's schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP on an object does not elevate the role to ACCOUNTADMIN or grant any schema-level or account-level privileges beyond the object itself.

options.text: The role can only view the object's access history
options.explanation: is incorrect. Viewing access history requires the GOVERNANCE_VIEWER or ACCOUNTADMIN role and the ACCOUNT_USAGE schema — OWNERSHIP on a specific object does not grant this.

options.text: The role has full control of the object including the ability to grant and transfer ownership
options.explanation: is correct. OWNERSHIP is the most powerful privilege on an individual object. The owning role can ALTER, DROP, and GRANT privileges on the object to other roles, and can transfer ownership. Only one role holds OWNERSHIP at a time.

questions.stem: Which system role is commonly associated with managing security grants and security objects?

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. ORGADMIN operates at the organization level for account management — it does not manage in-account security grants.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is the default lowest-privilege role that all users automatically receive; it has no security administration capabilities.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN manages database objects (tables, schemas, warehouses) but is not the designated role for managing security grants and access policies.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. SECURITYADMIN is the built-in role focused on security management. It can manage grants on all objects, create and manage users and roles (like USERADMIN), and is the recommended role for day-to-day access control administration.

questions.stem: A table contains sensitive salary data that should appear masked to most analysts but be visible to a privileged payroll role. Which feature is most appropriate?

options.text: Dynamic data masking policy
options.explanation: is correct. A dynamic data masking policy defines how a column's value is transformed based on the querying role. When the payroll role queries the column it sees real values; all other roles see a masked representation (e.g., fixed string or NULL).

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A row access policy filters which rows a role can see — it does not obfuscate column values within visible rows.

options.text: Warehouse resource monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. A warehouse resource monitor controls credit consumption — it has nothing to do with column-level data visibility.

options.text: Search optimization service
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search optimization speeds up selective point-lookup queries — it has no column-level security function.

questions.stem: Which statement about future grants is correct?

options.text: They replace the need for roles in the RBAC model
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants are a mechanism for automating privilege assignment within the RBAC model; they do not replace roles.

options.text: They retroactively fix ownership conflicts on existing objects only
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants apply prospectively to objects created after the grant is defined — they do not retroactively change privileges on objects that already exist.

options.text: They automatically grant specified privileges on new objects created in a schema or database
options.explanation: is correct. Future grants (`GRANT <privilege> ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ...`) tell Snowflake to automatically apply the specified privilege to any new objects of that type created in the specified scope going forward. This avoids the need to manually grant access after every new object is created.

options.text: They are available only for network policies and cannot be applied to tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants work for many object types including tables, views, schemas, and more — they are not limited to network policies.

questions.stem: What is the safest general approach when granting privileges to end users in Snowflake?

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all service accounts to avoid permission errors in pipelines
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting ACCOUNTADMIN to service accounts over-privileges them and violates least privilege — service accounts need only the specific privileges their workload requires.

options.text: Avoid using secondary roles in every design to reduce RBAC complexity
options.explanation: is incorrect. Secondary roles are a legitimate mechanism for users to combine privileges from multiple roles in a single session; avoiding them entirely may unnecessarily restrict what users can do in complex scenarios.

options.text: Grant privileges directly to individual users whenever possible
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting privileges directly to individual users bypasses the role hierarchy and makes auditing and revocation extremely difficult to maintain at scale.

options.text: Grant roles to users and assign privileges to roles, not directly to users
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake best practice is to grant privileges to roles (not directly to users) and then assign those roles to users. This makes privilege reviews, revocation, and auditing far more manageable — changing a role's privileges affects all users with that role at once.

questions.stem: A provider wants to give another Snowflake account read access to selected datasets without granting any internal administrative access. Which feature is most relevant?

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy controls IP-based access to Snowflake authentication — it does not grant or manage data access for another account.

options.text: Secure data sharing
options.explanation: is correct. Secure data sharing allows a provider to share specific databases, schemas, tables, or views with a consumer account. Data is not copied — the consumer queries the provider's storage directly using their own compute. No administrative access to the provider's account is granted.

options.text: Failover group
options.explanation: is incorrect. A failover group replicates data to a secondary account for disaster recovery; it is not designed to grant selective read access to an external partner.

options.text: Resource monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor limits credit consumption on warehouses — it has nothing to do with granting cross-account data access.

questions.stem: Which command family is used to inspect effective grants on an object or role?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS
options.explanation: is correct. `SHOW GRANTS ON <object>` and `SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE <role>` are the standard commands for inspecting what privileges have been granted on an object or to a role. This is the primary tool for access control auditing.

options.text: SHOW TASKS
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SHOW TASKS` lists task objects in the current schema — it does not display privilege grants.

options.text: SHOW WAREHOUSES
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SHOW WAREHOUSES` lists warehouse objects and their properties — it does not display privilege grants.

options.text: SHOW STAGES
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SHOW STAGES` lists stage objects — it does not display privilege information.

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that correctly reflect secure view behavior and governance in Snowflake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Secure views automatically grant OWNERSHIP on the underlying tables to all consumers
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating a secure view grants nothing on the underlying tables. In fact, a key purpose of secure views is to abstract consumers away from the underlying tables entirely.

options.text: Secure views are designed to hide underlying query logic better than regular views, especially in sharing scenarios
options.explanation: is correct. Secure views prevent the query optimizer from exposing the underlying table structure or view definition to users who only have access to the view — this is especially important in data sharing scenarios where consumers should not see the provider's data model.

options.text: Access to a secure view still requires the querying role to have the appropriate SELECT privilege on the view
options.explanation: is correct. Even a secure view requires that the querying role holds SELECT on the view. The 'secure' designation protects implementation details; it does not bypass Snowflake's standard privilege enforcement.

options.text: Secure views replace the need for masking policies in every governance scenario
options.explanation: is incorrect. Secure views and masking policies serve different purposes. Masking policies obfuscate column values dynamically based on role context within a view or table; secure views hide the view definition. Both may be needed in a complete governance solution.

questions.stem: A team wants row-level restrictions so regional managers see only records for their own region. Which Snowflake feature is designed for that purpose?

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: is correct. Row access policies define a filter function that controls which rows a given role (or session context) can see. When applied to a table, Snowflake transparently injects the filter, so each manager's query automatically returns only their region's rows.

options.text: Reader account
options.explanation: is incorrect. A reader account is a managed Snowflake account for external consumers — it does not implement row-level filtering within a table.

options.text: Warehouse AUTO_RESUME
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse AUTO_RESUME controls whether a suspended warehouse starts automatically on query arrival — it has nothing to do with row visibility.

options.text: Search optimization service
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search optimization accelerates highly selective queries — it is a performance feature, not a row-level security control.

questions.stem: Why should administrators be careful when using `WITH GRANT OPTION` in a privilege grant?

options.text: It encrypts the object's storage keys twice, which reduces query performance
options.explanation: is incorrect. `WITH GRANT OPTION` is purely a privilege delegation setting — it has no effect on storage encryption or query performance.

options.text: It allows the recipient role to further delegate that privilege to other roles, expanding access paths
options.explanation: is correct. `WITH GRANT OPTION` allows the recipient of a privilege to re-grant that same privilege to other roles. This can unintentionally create chains of privilege delegation that are hard to track and revoke, widening access beyond the original intent.

options.text: It is required for any read access and must always be included in SELECT grants
options.explanation: is incorrect. SELECT grants do not require `WITH GRANT OPTION`. A plain `GRANT SELECT ON TABLE t TO ROLE r` gives read access without allowing re-delegation.

options.text: It converts the receiving role into a new user object
options.explanation: is incorrect. `WITH GRANT OPTION` modifies how a privilege grant can be delegated; it does not create or transform any object or user.

questions.stem: What is a common reason to design a custom role hierarchy in Snowflake?

options.text: To disable all system-defined roles such as SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. System-defined roles (ACCOUNTADMIN, SYSADMIN, SECURITYADMIN, etc.) cannot be disabled. Custom roles supplement them within the hierarchy.

options.text: To increase warehouse size automatically when new roles are created
options.explanation: is incorrect. Role creation has no effect on warehouse sizing. Warehouse size is controlled separately through `ALTER WAREHOUSE` commands or warehouse properties.

options.text: To align privilege sets with organizational job functions and support delegated administration
options.explanation: is correct. Custom role hierarchies let administrators create roles that map to real job functions (e.g., ANALYST_ROLE, ETL_ROLE, DATA_STEWARD_ROLE) and nest them so higher-level roles inherit the privileges of lower-level ones. This simplifies privilege management and delegation.

options.text: To avoid using schemas inside databases
options.explanation: is incorrect. Schemas are database organizational objects that remain necessary regardless of role design decisions.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a network policy in Snowflake?

options.text: Restricting allowed client IP addresses for Snowflake authentication
options.explanation: is correct. A network policy defines allowed (and optionally blocked) IP address ranges. When activated at the account or user level, Snowflake rejects authentication attempts from IP addresses not in the allowed list.

options.text: Resizing virtual warehouses based on network latency
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse sizing is a compute configuration set through `ALTER WAREHOUSE`; it has nothing to do with network policies.

options.text: Creating zero-copy clones of databases
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy clones are created with the `CREATE ... CLONE` DDL command — network policies do not create clones.

options.text: Scheduling periodic task execution
options.explanation: is incorrect. Task scheduling is configured on TASK objects using CRON or minute-interval schedules — network policies do not control task execution.

questions.stem: Which feature should an administrator enable to require an additional verification factor for interactive users?

options.text: Search optimization service
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search optimization service is a performance feature that accelerates selective queries on large tables — it is not an authentication control.

options.text: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
options.explanation: is correct. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requires users to provide a second verification factor (such as a TOTP code from an authenticator app) in addition to their password. Snowflake supports MFA enrollment through Duo Security and administrators can enforce it via authentication policies.

options.text: Automatic clustering
options.explanation: is incorrect. Automatic clustering maintains micro-partition order on frequently queried columns for query performance — it is unrelated to authentication.

options.text: Snowpipe Streaming
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpipe Streaming is a data ingestion capability for low-latency streaming loads — not an authentication security control.

questions.stem: A company wants private connectivity so traffic between Snowflake and its cloud environment does not traverse the public internet. Which concept is most relevant?

options.text: Temporary tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Temporary tables are transient in-session data objects — they have no effect on the network path used to connect to Snowflake.

options.text: Private connectivity such as AWS PrivateLink, Azure Private Link, or Google Cloud Private Service Connect
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake supports private connectivity through cloud-provider-specific mechanisms (AWS PrivateLink, Azure Private Link, Google Cloud Private Service Connect). These create a private network path between the customer's VPC/VNet and Snowflake's service endpoints so traffic never traverses the public internet.

options.text: Materialized views
options.explanation: is incorrect. Materialized views are precomputed result sets that accelerate certain query patterns — they are a performance feature, not a network security feature.

options.text: Change Data Capture streams
options.explanation: is incorrect. Change Data Capture streams track DML changes on tables for incremental pipeline processing — they do not control network connectivity.

questions.stem: What is a key reason to regularly rotate keys or credentials for Snowflake service integrations?

options.text: To reduce exposure if credentials are compromised and maintain security hygiene
options.explanation: is correct. Regular credential rotation limits the window of exposure if a key or credential is compromised. Even if an attacker obtains a credential, rotation ensures it becomes invalid before significant damage can occur. This is standard security hygiene for service accounts.

options.text: To improve micro-partition pruning for large tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Micro-partition pruning is a storage optimization that depends on data clustering and metadata — credential rotation has no effect on it.

options.text: To increase the result cache hit rate for repeated queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result cache behavior depends on query identity and data freshness — credential rotation does not influence caching.

options.text: To enable zero-copy cloning on schemas
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy cloning is triggered by a `CREATE ... CLONE` DDL command — it is unrelated to credential management.

questions.stem: Which statement about federated authentication (SSO) in Snowflake is most accurate?

options.text: SSO is only available for reader accounts and not for full Snowflake accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. SSO is supported for standard Snowflake accounts — it is not restricted to reader accounts.

options.text: SSO replaces Snowflake's RBAC privilege model entirely
options.explanation: is incorrect. SSO is an authentication mechanism, not an authorization model. RBAC (roles and privileges) still governs what authenticated users can access. SSO and RBAC complement each other.

options.text: SSO centralizes authentication with an external identity provider and can improve user lifecycle management
options.explanation: is correct. Federated authentication (SSO) integrates Snowflake with an external IdP (such as Okta or Entra ID) via SAML 2.0. Authentication is delegated to the IdP, which centralizes credential management, allows existing corporate MFA and provisioning workflows to apply, and simplifies user lifecycle management.

options.text: SSO can only be enabled on weekends during maintenance windows
options.explanation: is incorrect. SSO can be configured at any time — there is no maintenance-window restriction.

questions.stem: After updating a network policy, several users report they can no longer sign in. What should an administrator verify first?

options.text: Whether the users' warehouse was suspended before they tried to connect
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse suspension affects query execution, not login authentication. A user can log in even when all warehouses are suspended.

options.text: Whether a table owner was changed, blocking the session from starting
options.explanation: is incorrect. Table ownership changes affect privilege grants on those tables but have no effect on whether a user can authenticate to the account.

options.text: Whether the users' client IP addresses fall within the allowed range defined by the updated policy
options.explanation: is correct. A network policy blocks authentication for clients whose IP addresses are not in the allowed list. After a policy change, the first thing to check is whether the affected users' IP ranges (corporate VPN, remote offices, etc.) are still permitted in the updated policy.

options.text: Whether the result cache was invalidated by a concurrent DML statement
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result cache invalidation affects whether a cached result is reused — it plays no role in blocking authentication.

questions.stem: Select TWO controls that most directly strengthen authentication security for interactive Snowflake users. (Select TWO)

options.text: Increasing virtual warehouse size
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse size is a compute configuration — it has no effect on how users authenticate to Snowflake.

options.text: MFA (multi-factor authentication)
options.explanation: is correct. MFA adds a second verification factor beyond a password, making it significantly harder for an attacker to gain access even with stolen credentials.

options.text: SSO with a trusted corporate identity provider
options.explanation: is correct. Federated SSO with a trusted corporate IdP centralizes authentication, allows existing corporate MFA/conditional access policies to apply, and typically uses stronger credential management than Snowflake-native passwords alone.

options.text: Using longer VARCHAR column types for user-entered data
options.explanation: is incorrect. VARCHAR column size is a data type setting for table columns — it is completely unrelated to user authentication security.

questions.stem: Why might an administrator configure key-pair authentication for a Snowflake service account?

options.text: To support non-interactive, password-free authentication in automation and pipeline tooling
options.explanation: is correct. Key-pair authentication uses a private/public RSA key pair instead of a password. This is well suited for service accounts used by pipelines, Terraform, or Snowflake drivers (Python, JDBC, etc.) because there is no plaintext password to manage in secrets vaults or CI/CD systems.

options.text: To enable automatic clustering on tables queried by the service account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Automatic clustering is a table maintenance feature configured per-table with `ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY`; it is completely independent of how the service account authenticates.

options.text: To reduce Fail-safe storage costs for objects owned by the service account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Fail-safe is a platform-level data recovery mechanism for permanent tables. Authentication method does not influence storage fail-safe periods.

options.text: To replace all network policies assigned to the service account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Key-pair authentication is a credential mechanism; it does not alter or replace network policies governing which IPs can connect.

questions.stem: What best describes how Snowflake handles data encryption?

options.text: Snowflake provides transparent encryption for data at rest and in transit as a built-in platform capability
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake encrypts all data at rest using AES-256 and encrypts data in transit using TLS. This is provided transparently as part of the managed service across all editions — customers do not need to implement encryption themselves.

options.text: Customers must implement all encryption manually by applying column-level functions to every table
options.explanation: is incorrect. While Snowflake supports customer-managed encryption through Tri-Secret Secure, basic encryption is handled automatically by the platform — manual column-level encryption by every customer is not required.

options.text: Enabling encryption automatically disables cross-region replication for encrypted objects
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encryption has no effect on replication capability. Encrypted data can be replicated normally through replication and failover groups.

options.text: Encryption at rest is available only for Enterprise Edition; Standard Edition stores data unencrypted
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encryption at rest and in transit is provided across all Snowflake editions, not just Enterprise. Only features like Tri-Secret Secure (BYOK) are edition-restricted.

questions.stem: A security review asks for evidence of who accessed sensitive objects recently. Which Snowflake data source is most directly relevant?

options.text: Warehouse AUTO_SUSPEND configuration settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_SUSPEND is a warehouse property that controls idle shutdown timing — it does not record who accessed data objects.

options.text: File format definitions in INFORMATION_SCHEMA
options.explanation: is incorrect. File format definitions describe how data files are parsed during load/unload; they contain no user access records.

options.text: Zero-copy clone creation metadata
options.explanation: is incorrect. Clone metadata tracks when clones were created and from what source — it does not record ongoing data access by users.

options.text: ACCESS_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE
options.explanation: is correct. The ACCESS_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE records which users (and their roles) accessed which objects and when — including column-level read access and write operations. It is specifically designed for the kind of access audit evidence a security review requires.

questions.stem: What is zero-copy cloning primarily used for in Snowflake?

options.text: Permanently archiving data to external cloud storage via COPY INTO
options.explanation: is incorrect. Permanent archiving to external storage uses features like COPY INTO to external stages — not zero-copy cloning.

options.text: Enforcing MFA on users who query a cloned object
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is an authentication setting applied to users — it has no relationship to whether objects are clones.

options.text: Creating fast logical copies of objects for dev/test or recovery without duplicating underlying storage immediately
options.explanation: is correct. Zero-copy cloning creates a metadata pointer to the same underlying micro-partitions as the source object. No data is physically copied at clone time, making it nearly instantaneous. As changes are made to the clone or source, only the changed micro-partitions diverge in storage.

options.text: Disabling Time Travel retention on the cloned database to reduce cost
options.explanation: is incorrect. Cloned objects inherit the source's Time Travel setting by default; cloning does not disable Time Travel.

questions.stem: Which table type is often chosen when lower-cost storage is acceptable and Fail-safe recovery is not required?

options.text: Permanent table with maximum Time Travel and 7-day Fail-safe
options.explanation: is incorrect. A permanent table with maximum Time Travel (90 days for Enterprise) combined with the 7-day Fail-safe period maximizes storage cost — the opposite of the goal.

options.text: Secure view
options.explanation: is incorrect. A secure view is a view with implementation detail protection — it is not a table type and stores no data independently.

options.text: External table referencing S3 or Azure Blob storage
options.explanation: is incorrect. An external table references data in an external stage (S3/Azure/GCS) — it stores no data in Snowflake and has different use cases around not copying data into Snowflake.

options.text: Transient table
options.explanation: is correct. Transient tables have a maximum Time Travel retention period of 1 day and no Fail-safe period (compared to 7 days for permanent tables). This makes them appropriate for staging or intermediate data that does not need full recovery protection, reducing associated storage costs.

questions.stem: What is Snowflake Time Travel used for?

options.text: Querying or restoring historical data versions within the configured retention period
options.explanation: is correct. Time Travel allows users to query data as it existed at a past point in time using `AT` or `BEFORE` syntax, clone objects from a historical state, and restore dropped objects — all within the configured retention window (up to 90 days for Enterprise Edition).

options.text: Scaling multi-cluster warehouses for peak concurrency
options.explanation: is incorrect. Scaling for peak concurrency is addressed by multi-cluster warehouse configuration — not by Time Travel.

options.text: Configuring MFA enrollment for interactive users
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA enrollment is managed through the Snowflake user settings and authentication policy — Time Travel has no relationship to authentication.

options.text: Creating and managing network rules for outbound traffic
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network rules for outbound traffic are defined using Snowflake's NETWORK RULE objects and associated with network policies — not through Time Travel.

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowflake Fail-safe is correct at a high level?

options.text: Fail-safe is a warehouse scaling mode that activates when all clusters are busy
options.explanation: is incorrect. Fail-safe is a storage recovery feature, not a compute scaling mode. Multi-cluster warehouse auto-scale handles the scenario of all clusters being busy.

options.text: Fail-safe applies to temporary tables to provide a low-cost recovery option
options.explanation: is incorrect. Temporary and transient tables do not have a Fail-safe period — that is precisely why they cost less to store.

options.text: Fail-safe is an additional Snowflake-managed recovery period for permanent tables that begins after Time Travel expires
options.explanation: is correct. After a permanent table's Time Travel window expires, Snowflake retains data for an additional 7-day Fail-safe period. During Fail-safe, data can only be recovered by Snowflake Support — it is not user-accessible but provides a last-resort recovery option.

options.text: Fail-safe lets end users self-serve data recovery by running UNDROP statements at any time
options.explanation: is incorrect. During Fail-safe, recovery is performed by Snowflake Support on behalf of the customer — end users cannot self-serve Fail-safe recovery. Self-service recovery uses Time Travel (UNDROP or `AT`/`BEFORE` queries).

questions.stem: A large table has queries that often filter on a small subset of column values. Which physical concept most directly supports pruning efficiency in Snowflake?

options.text: Network policies applied to the table's owning schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network policies control authentication IP restrictions — they have no effect on how table storage is scanned.

options.text: Reader account configuration for the table's consumers
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader accounts are external consumer accounts for data sharing — their configuration does not affect how data is stored or pruned in the provider's account.

options.text: Micro-partitions and their per-partition column metadata
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake stores table data in micro-partitions (typically 50–500 MB compressed) and maintains metadata about the min/max values of each column per partition. When a query has a filter, Snowflake uses this metadata to skip (prune) entire micro-partitions that cannot satisfy the predicate, scanning far less data.

options.text: MFA enforcement on roles that query the table
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is an authentication security control — it has absolutely no bearing on how Snowflake scans or prunes table storage.

questions.stem: What is a likely use case for the Snowflake search optimization service?

options.text: Improving performance of highly selective point-lookups on large tables
options.explanation: is correct. Search optimization service builds a persistent search access path for highly selective queries — for example, `WHERE email = 'user@example.com'` on a billion-row table. It is most valuable when queries return a small fraction of rows and standard micro-partition pruning is insufficient.

options.text: Replacing RBAC row-level security policies with index-based filtering
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search optimization is a performance feature, not a security or access control mechanism. Row-level security is implemented with row access policies, which are unrelated to search optimization.

options.text: Automatically suspending idle warehouses after a configurable timeout
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_SUSPEND on the warehouse handles automatic suspension of idle compute — search optimization has no effect on warehouse lifecycle.

options.text: Replicating databases across regions for disaster recovery
options.explanation: is incorrect. Cross-region database replication is handled by replication groups and failover groups, not by search optimization.

questions.stem: Which operation creates an independent logical copy of a Snowflake database at a point in time for experimentation?

options.text: Suspend the warehouse that owns the database
options.explanation: is incorrect. Suspending a warehouse stops compute billing — it does not create a copy of any database and has no effect on storage structure.

options.text: Clone the database using CREATE DATABASE ... CLONE
options.explanation: is correct. `CREATE DATABASE dev_db CLONE prod_db` creates a zero-copy clone of the entire database — including all schemas, tables, views, and other objects — at the current point in time. The clone is independent; changes to the clone do not affect the source.

options.text: Alter the file format used to load the database's tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Altering a file format changes how data files are parsed during load or unload — it does not create a database copy.

options.text: Create a network rule scoped to the database
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network rules govern inbound or outbound traffic for network policies — they are not related to creating database copies.

questions.stem: Why might an administrator use a transient schema for staging workflow tables?

options.text: Transient schemas require MFA for all queries against their tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Transient schemas have no effect on authentication requirements. MFA is a user-level authentication setting, not a schema attribute.

options.text: Transient schemas automatically increase warehouse concurrency for ETL jobs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse concurrency is controlled by warehouse size and multi-cluster settings — schema type has no influence on concurrency.

options.text: Transient schemas eliminate Fail-safe storage overhead for intermediate data that does not need that protection
options.explanation: is correct. Tables in a transient schema inherit transient behavior: no Fail-safe period and a maximum 1-day Time Travel. For staging data that is re-loaded on each ETL run and does not need long-term recovery, this avoids the additional storage cost of Fail-safe.

options.text: Transient schemas replace streams and tasks for change data capture pipelines
options.explanation: is incorrect. Streams and tasks are CDC and orchestration features. Transient schemas do not replicate or replace this functionality.

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that are generally true about the relationship between warehouses and storage in Snowflake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Warehouse size controls the Fail-safe retention window on permanent tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Fail-safe retention (7 days for permanent objects) is a fixed platform behavior — it is not configurable and is not affected by warehouse size.

options.text: Virtual warehouses provide compute resources while data storage resides separately in cloud object storage
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake separates compute from storage. Virtual warehouses are ephemeral compute clusters; data in databases is stored durably in cloud object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) independently of any warehouse state.

options.text: Suspending a warehouse does not delete or affect data stored in databases
options.explanation: is correct. Suspending a warehouse stops the compute clusters and billing for that warehouse. All data in Snowflake databases persists in cloud storage completely unaffected — it can be queried again as soon as any warehouse resumes.

options.text: Databases stop existing and become inaccessible when their associated warehouse is suspended
options.explanation: is incorrect. Databases are independent of warehouses. A database continues to exist with all its data when a warehouse is suspended — it simply cannot execute queries until a warehouse is available.

questions.stem: A table is accidentally modified and the team wants to restore it to an earlier state within its retention period. Which Snowflake feature is the first place to look?

options.text: Resource monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor tracks and limits credit consumption — it has no data recovery capability.

options.text: Time Travel
options.explanation: is correct. Time Travel is the primary self-service mechanism for recovering accidentally modified or dropped data within the retention window. An administrator can use `CREATE TABLE ... CLONE ... BEFORE (STATEMENT => ...)` or `UNDROP TABLE` to restore the table.

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy controls authentication IP access — it cannot restore table data.

options.text: Reader account
options.explanation: is incorrect. A reader account is a data sharing construct for external consumers — it provides no data recovery functionality.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature most directly helps administrators review user sign-in activity?

options.text: LOGIN_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE
options.explanation: is correct. The LOGIN_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE captures authentication events — who logged in, from which IP, using which authentication method, and whether the attempt succeeded or failed. It is the primary source for reviewing sign-in activity.

options.text: MICRO_PARTITION_ROW_COUNTS view
options.explanation: is incorrect. MICRO_PARTITION_ROW_COUNTS is a storage metadata view about how many rows are in each micro-partition — it does not capture authentication events.

options.text: STAGES view in INFORMATION_SCHEMA
options.explanation: is incorrect. The STAGES view lists stage objects (named internal or external stages) — it has no user login information.

options.text: WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY view
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records warehouse lifecycle events (start, stop, resize) — not user authentication events.

questions.stem: What is a strong reason to centralize Snowflake audit monitoring?

options.text: Centralized audit monitoring automatically increases warehouse size to handle more queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Audit monitoring has no effect on warehouse sizing. Compute capacity is managed separately through warehouse configuration.

options.text: It removes the need for RBAC by consolidating access controls into one audit dashboard
options.explanation: is incorrect. Audit monitoring complements RBAC — it does not replace it. RBAC controls what access is allowed; audit monitoring records what access occurred.

options.text: It helps detect anomalous behavior patterns, support investigations, and demonstrate compliance more consistently
options.explanation: is correct. Centralizing audit data — combining login history, query history, and access history into a SIEM or monitoring platform — enables correlation of events across users and time. This makes it practical to detect anomalies, investigate incidents, and produce compliance evidence consistently.

options.text: It disables result caching to ensure all queries appear in audit logs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result-cached queries do still appear in QUERY_HISTORY with their cache-hit status noted. Disabling caching to inflate log volume would increase cost without improving audit quality.

questions.stem: A regulator asks for evidence that privileged roles are tightly controlled. Which practice provides the strongest evidence?

options.text: Using the largest warehouse size for all regulatory queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse size is a cost and performance consideration — it has no relevance to demonstrating that privileged roles are properly controlled.

options.text: Documented role design, limited assignment of powerful roles, and periodic access reviews with audit trail
options.explanation: is correct. Regulators expect to see a documented RBAC design that justifies why roles have the privileges they do, a list showing only a small number of named individuals hold powerful roles (ACCOUNTADMIN, SECURITYADMIN), and evidence of periodic reviews that catch and remove stale assignments.

options.text: Deleting old query history to reduce the attack surface for auditors
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deleting audit history is the opposite of what regulators want to see. Tampered or incomplete audit trails raise red flags and may constitute a compliance violation.

options.text: Disabling tags to simplify the governance object model
options.explanation: is incorrect. Tags support governance by classifying objects — removing them weakens the governance posture rather than providing evidence of control.

questions.stem: Which data source is most relevant when proving that a masking or row-access policy was part of the governance design?

options.text: Warehouse AUTO_RESUME configuration settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_RESUME is a warehouse property controlling whether a warehouse starts automatically when a query arrives — it has nothing to do with data governance policies.

options.text: Object definitions, policy attachments, and grants visible in Snowflake metadata and ACCOUNT_USAGE
options.explanation: is correct. To prove governance design, an auditor would inspect `SHOW MASKING POLICIES`, `SHOW ROW ACCESS POLICIES`, and the ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES view to see which policies exist, what rules they enforce, and which columns or tables they are attached to. This metadata is the definitive governance evidence.

options.text: CSV file format delimiter settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. CSV delimiter settings are file format properties used during data loading — they are completely unrelated to data security policies.

options.text: Task CRON schedule definitions only
options.explanation: is incorrect. Task CRON schedules define when automated SQL statements run — they do not show masking or row-access policy design.

questions.stem: Select TWO activities that fit a compliance-focused Snowflake administration practice. (Select TWO)

options.text: Granting ACCOUNTADMIN broadly so auditors can access everything without individual justification
options.explanation: is incorrect. Broad ACCOUNTADMIN grants undermine compliance by eliminating the least-privilege principle. Regulators specifically look for tightly controlled privileged access — broad grants are a major finding.

options.text: Regular review of access and audit history for anomalous or unauthorized activity
options.explanation: is correct. Regularly reviewing LOGIN_HISTORY, QUERY_HISTORY, and ACCESS_HISTORY for anomalies (unusual access times, unexpected data access volumes) is a core compliance control that demonstrates ongoing monitoring.

options.text: Applying governance tags and data classification policies to sensitive objects
options.explanation: is correct. Tagging sensitive objects and attaching masking or row-access policies provides documented, enforceable data classification — a key requirement in frameworks like GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2.

options.text: Disabling QUERY_HISTORY retention to reduce the volume of evidence available for review
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reducing audit evidence is the opposite of compliance. QUERY_HISTORY is a key audit trail; disabling or limiting it would create gaps in compliance evidence and could itself be a finding.

questions.stem: Why is immutable and durable audit evidence important in a Snowflake governance program?

options.text: It automatically improves micro-partition clustering depth over time
options.explanation: is incorrect. Micro-partition clustering is a storage optimization for query performance driven by data ingestion patterns or the CLUSTER BY setting — audit evidence has no effect on it.

options.text: It replaces the need for backup and recovery strategies
options.explanation: is incorrect. Backup and recovery addresses data durability in case of accidental deletion or corruption. Audit evidence provides accountability records — the two serve complementary but different purposes.

options.text: It supports trustworthy forensic investigations and external compliance reviews by proving who did what and when
options.explanation: is correct. Immutable audit evidence — logs that cannot be altered or deleted by insiders — is the foundation of forensic investigations and external audits. It allows organizations to prove with confidence that specific users performed (or did not perform) specific actions at specific times.

options.text: It eliminates the need for user security training programs
options.explanation: is incorrect. User security awareness training is a control unto itself. Audit evidence records behavior after training but does not replace the training that shapes that behavior.
